title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Premise
In the 1980s, Max Headroom appeared to be fully computer-generated.
Sharp. Synthetic. Digital.
But behind the illusion: - Human performance
- Manual compositing
- Crafted intelligence
The AI was not real.
The experience was.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Inversion
We take that concept and flip it.
Today, the intelligence is real.
But it exists on a spectrum: - From dufus
- To genius
- Often unpredictably in between
AI is powerful—but not yet consistently reliable.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Opportunity
What if clients could experience AI at its best…
Without being exposed to its worst?

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Concept
A cloud-based design studio where:

Clients interact with intelligent AI agents

Agents feel like autonomous creative partners

The experience is seamless, consistent, always on

A single, continuous digital presence.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Illusion (By Design)
The agent appears: - Fully capable
- Context-aware
- Creatively reliable
Like a modern digital persona.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Reality
Behind every agent:

Human designers

Creative directors

Strategists

Operating quietly in the loop.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Model
Human-in-the-loop AI

AI handles speed, iteration, continuity

Humans ensure:

Accuracy

Cultural relevance

Creative quality

Strategic alignment

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
Why This Matters
Pure AI: - Fast, but inconsistent
Pure human: - High quality, but slow and expensive
We combine both: - AI interface
- Human assurance

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Strategy
“Fake it till you make it”—but with intent.
Not deception.
Staged maturity.

Present a stable AI experience today

Use human oversight to:

Correct errors

Train systems

Refine outputs

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Evolution
Over time:

Less human correction

More autonomous capability

Increasing system intelligence

The illusion becomes reality.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Value
Clients get: - Always-on creative partner
- Faster turnaround
- Consistent interaction
- High-quality output
Without AI volatility.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Position
AI delivers the experience.
Humans guarantee the outcome.

title: The Visible Agent, The Invisible Studio
text:
The Vision
The Visible Agent.
The Invisible Studio.
A new model for creative work: - Scalable
- Intelligent
- Human-secured

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Welcome & Introduction
Seminar: AI Workflows for Academic Writing
Duration: 90 minutes
Focus: How AI supports — not replaces — scholarship.

Opening context:

Thesis writing challenges: information overload, argument structure, fatigue.
AI can assist with search, synthesis, and writing clarity.
You remain the scholar — AI is your assistant, not your author.

In this session you will:

Learn how to search, synthesise and draft with AI.
Explore ethics and attribution.
Leave with a personal workflow plan.




Greet participants warmly and thank them for joining.
Introduce the title: AI Workflows for Academic Writing.
Explain that the session is compact and hands-on.
Ask everyone to open the live poll or Mentimeter link.
Say: “Tell me your top writing pain point; we’ll use your answers for examples later.”
Mention that poll results will guide the live demos.
Transition: “Let’s look at where AI actually fits into academic work.”
Slide Purpose: Orientation and motivation.
Learning Outcomes addressed: introduction to LO 1–3 orientation.
Facilitator activity: read script.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
What frustrates you most?


  


https://forms.gle/oSL8rGdxmhjioe4x8




Ask everyone to open the live poll or Mentimeter link.
Say: “Tell me your top writing pain point; we’ll use your answers for examples later.”
Mention that poll results will guide the live demos.
Transition: “Let’s look at where AI actually fits into academic work.”
Facilitator activity: launch live poll, note top three issues for later demos.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Where AI Helps…  and Where It Doesn’t (Yet)

University standards require:

Integrity — no plagiarism or data fabrication.
Originality — ideas must be your own.
Transparency — disclose AI use if required by policy.

AI can assist with:

Rapid literature discovery

Structuring outlines, abstracts, and drafts

Grammar, tone, and citation checks.

Generating sample code or figures from your notes.

AI cannot replace:

Reading and understanding papers.

Critical judgment or design reasoning.

Ethical authorship or data verification.








Tip


Use AI to speed tasks — not to skip thinking.







Where AI Helps — and Where It Doesn’t.
Explain that AI tools act like accelerators, not replacements for academic skills.
Give one concrete example: “If you ask for key papers, always verify titles and DOIs before citing.”
Mention that AI may misread visuals or produce generic text if prompts are vague.
Emphasise that the human remains the researcher; AI does the mechanical side.
Conclude: “We’ll explore how to balance speed and scholarship in today’s session.”
Segment timing: 5–15 min
Learning Outcomes linked: LO #1 (Search & curate), LO #2 (Synthesize literature), LO #3 (Draft with AI) — Knowledge phase
Facilitator task: deliver a 10-minute talk with examples, show a one-page cheat-sheet or diagram of the research lifecycle.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
What’s your research about?

Activity:
In pairs, take turns to describe your current research question.
Prompting Q’s:

What are you studying?

Where are you stuck: finding sources, structuring ideas, or writing?

What would you like AI to help with today?

Timing:

Each pair talk to each other → switch sides after 3 minutes.

Post a 3-sentence summary of your project in the chat.




Announce that it’s time to meet one partner for a quick exchange.

Say: “In your breakout pair, tell each other what you’re researching and what frustrates you most right now.”

Encourage short, focused sharing — one minute setup, one minute response.

After the chat posts appear, read two or three aloud as examples.

Note recurring themes — these will guide which examples you demo later.

Transition line: “Let’s see how we can use AI to find relevant papers for these topics.”
Segment timing: 15–25 min
Learning Outcomes linked: LO #2 (Synthesize literature) — motivation and peer context.
Format: Breakout pairs → plenary chat.
Deliverable: 2-sentence problem statement per participant.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #1: Search & Curate Literature
Find 10 peer-reviewed papers relevant to your topic within 20 minutes.

Activity:

Create the prompt - context is king!

Frame your topic (Boolean+)
Filter for recent peer-reviewed items (2018+)
Check quality on publisher sites (DOI addresses).
Generate a 1-sentence annotation for each paper.
Format result as an ordered list.

Paste your list (10 items + summaries) in chat/shared doc.
Verify one paper: check DOI link to confirm accuracy.




Introduce this as the first hands-on demo of AI in research.

Remind them to treat AI suggestions as leads, not truth.

Explain that we’ll build an annotated bibliography later from these results.

Model a live search using a neutral example topic (e.g., sustainable packaging design).

Walk through each result: check title, author, journal, year.

Ask participants to verify one entry before posting their list.

Summarise: “We’ve just completed the first outcome — a curated, checked mini-bibliography.”

Segment timing: 25 – 40 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #1 (Search & curate). Format: Demo + guided individual practice. Deliverable: Five-paper annotated mini-bibliography (shared text or chat).

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Frame your topic


Formulate Old School Boolean Search
Combine key terms to capture relevant papers and exclude noise using Boolean building blocks








Op.
Function
Example




OR
related terms
“sustainable product” OR “ecodesign”


AND
combine diff. ideas
“ecodesign” AND “manufacturing”


” “
exact phrase
“green design”


*
find word variants
design* → design, designer, designing



 resulting in… 

(“sustainable product” OR “green design”) AND (design* OR develop* OR engineer*)

 Paste into Google Scholar, Scopus, or LLM:
Apply filters: Peer-reviewed, 2018 onwards, English language etc
Frame Your Topic with AI (Boolean+)

Generate a Boolean search string for my topic:
[insert your topic here].

Include:
    •   Synonyms and related terms (use OR)
    •   Two distinct concept groups combined with AND
    •   Quotation marks for multi-word phrases
    •   Wildcards where useful (e.g., design*)

Return the Boolean query on one line ready for Scopus, Google Scholar, or Elicit.




Start by asking: “What are the key ideas in your topic?” (e.g., sustainability + design).

Explain that OR collects synonyms and broadens results; AND combines concept groups.

Show that quotation marks keep multi-word phrases intact.

Demonstrate the asterisk for word variations – “design*” captures design, designer, designing.

Run the example live in a database or AI tool.

Emphasise that Boolean logic helps you control results – it’s not random guessing.

Transition: “Now that we’ve built a good search string, we can feed it into AI tools to generate candidate papers.”

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Creating Prompts
Build and refine LLM prompts for literature discovery.

What is a prompt?
A short instruction or question you give to an AI model.
It guides the model’s task, scope, and format of response.

Find 10 peer-reviewed papers from 2018–2025  
on sustainable or green product design, development, or engineering.  
Include title, author, year, DOI, and one-line summary.  
Mark any uncertain citation with [VERIFY].

Prompt structure

Role – tell the AI who it should act as.

Task – describe exactly what to do.

Context / criteria – add limits or filters.

Output format – specify how results should appear.

Optimise Your Prompt with the LLM
You can also ask the LLM to co-create or optimise the prompt with you.

You are an academic search assistant helping me locate recent, peer-reviewed papers.
Improve my prompt for clarity and reproducibility.

Keep all key terms and add filters for:
    •   peer-reviewed journals
    •   date range (2018–present)
    •   output format (table)
Return your optimised version.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Build and refine LLM prompts for literature discovery.

Detailed Prompt Sample

You are a research assistant helping me locate recent, peer-reviewed papers.
Topic focus: [YOUR TOPIC]

Please do the following:
1. Return a list of **10 peer-reviewed journal articles from 2018 onwards**.  
2. For each article, include:
   - Title  
   - Author(s)  
   - Year  
   - DOI or publisher link  
   - A **one-sentence summary** of the study’s aim or contribution.  
3. Exclude:
   - Preprints, blogs, or non-peer-reviewed sources  
   - Duplicates or incomplete references  
4. If uncertain about any citation, mark it with `[VERIFY]` instead of guessing a DOI.  
5. Present the output as a simple table.

Example output format:
| # | Title | Author(s) | Year | DOI | Summary |  
|---|--------|------------|------|------|----------|  
| 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

Respond only with verifiable results.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #2: Synthesize the Literature & find the Gap

Short Prompt:
Write a 150–200 word paragraph summarising what’s known + the gap.
Sample Prompt:

Using these 10 papers, write a 150-word paragraph that\
(a) summarises the main approaches,
(b) identifies what remains unresolved,
(c) ends with the gap my project will address.

Your task (10 min):

Create your own prompt.

Draft your paragraph.

Paste it in the shared document/chat.

Label your post: “LIT-GAP PARA – [Your Name].”

Checklist:

Clarity
Evidence from sources
Explicit gap statement




Announce that it’s time to turn search results into synthesis.

Encourage writing first in plain language, then refining with AI if they wish.

Remind them to cite or note authors within the paragraph.

Say: “Keep your paragraph factual and show where the gap is—don’t just list papers.”

After 10 minutes, invite two volunteers to read theirs aloud.

Transition: “We’ll now swap for quick peer feedback to see how these paragraphs read to others.”

Segment timing: 40 – 50 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #2 (Synthesize literature) Format: Individual writing → shared submission Deliverable: 150–200 word literature-gap paragraph per participant

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Rapid Peer Feedback
(Small-Group Critique)

Task:
Strengthen clarity and logic in your literature-gap paragraph.
Group setup:
3 participants per group.
Task (10 min total):

Exchange your paragraphs.
Give 4 comments each:

Is the prompt well-structured?

Is the paragraph clear and concise?

Is the evidence properly linked to sources?

Is the gap explicit and meaningful?


Quick rubric (1–5 scale):
1 = unclear 3 = understandable 5 = compelling
Deliverable:
3 short comments + rating per peer.



Direct students to breakout groups of three.

Say: “Each person reads another’s paragraph and gives three quick, specific comments.”

Emphasise constructive tone—focus on clarity and connection to literature.

Display the 1–5 scale so everyone uses a common measure.

Keep time: 3 minutes each round, one minute to wrap up.

After 10 minutes, call everyone back and ask for one insight from the activity.

Transition: “Now that we’ve practised synthesis, let’s try generating and refining an abstract using AI.”

Segment timing: 50 – 60 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #2 (Synthesize literature) — peer feedback and revision. Format: Small groups of three. Deliverable: Peer comments and numeric rating for each paragraph.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #3: From Draft to Abstract

Create an Abstract with AI:
Produce a ≤ 250-word abstract using an AI-assisted prompt, then edit for clarity and accuracy.
Demo steps (facilitator):

Paste a 1-page methods + results section into the LLM.

Run the “Structured Abstract” prompt.



Create a structured abstract (≤ 250 words) from this text.
Keep numbers and claims as given, and
mark any you’re unsure of with [CHECK].


Review the output for structure (IMRaD form – Intro, Methods, Results, Discussion).

Run a second prompt to humanise tone and insert [CHECK] tags where facts need verification.
Paste your before/after pair in the shared document.

Edit Checklist:

Accuracy
Clarity
Logical flow
Word limit ≤ 250




Introduce this as our “AI draft and refine” moment.

Demonstrate live with a neutral sample text (e.g., study on design thinking methods).

Show how to run the first prompt and what a structured abstract looks like.

Emphasise human editing afterwards — highlight where AI misses nuance or adds generic phrasing.

Ask participants to try the prompt with their own data if they have it, or use the sample provided.

Discuss how the [CHECK] tags help track verification needs.

Transition: “Next, we’ll look at how to handle ethics and AI use in real publication scenarios.”

Segment timing: 60 – 70 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #3 (Draft with AI). Format: Live demo + short individual practice. Deliverable: Before/after abstract pair + reflection checklist.

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Ethics in Practice — Hallucination & Attribution Challenge

Goal (LO #7 – Ethics & Attribution):
Identify ethical responses when AI invents or misattributes sources.
Scenario:
An AI tool suggests a “seminal” paper that doesn’t exist.
Your co-author wants to include it to strengthen your argument.
Group Task (10 min):

Discuss → What 3 steps would you take to verify and respond?

Draft → One sentence your lab could add to its SOP to prevent this.

Post → Your SOP sentence + 3 steps in the shared doc.

Reference Checklist:

Search the DOI or publisher site
Cross-check citations
Disclose AI use honestly




Read the scenario aloud to set the tone.

Emphasise that “hallucination” means a fabricated reference or data point.

Instruct small groups (3–4 people) to identify a verification sequence—what would they do first?

Suggest steps like: confirm DOI, find real sources, note corrections transparently.

After 8 minutes, call them back for 2-minute sharebacks.

Summarise common themes: verification, documentation, disclosure.

Transition: “Let’s hear a few of your lab policy statements before we wrap up.”

Segment timing: 70 – 82 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #7 (Ethics & Attribution) Format: Small-group scenario discussion + plenary shareback Deliverable: 1-sentence lab SOP statement + 3 verification steps posted to shared doc

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Sharebacks

Consolidate insights.

Read your 1-sentence SOP statement. (voluntary)

What are good practices? For instance:

Always verify sources manually.

Keep an audit trail of AI prompts and outputs.

Use transparent disclosure language.





Invite 2–3 groups to share their SOP statements aloud.

Affirm consistent points (verification, disclosure, authorship).

Encourage quick clarifying questions from the group.

Keep responses concise; flag deeper issues for follow-up resources.

Transition line: “We’ll finish by capturing the key take-aways and linking them back to the outcomes we began with.”
“The purpose of this exercise is not to avoid AI, but to use it with integrity and precision.”

Segment timing: 40 min Learning Outcomes linked: Reinforces LO #7 (Ethics & Attribution) and transitions to LO #8 (Workflow Design). Format: Plenary shareback + facilitated discussion Deliverable: Shared SOP statements and verbal Q&A

title: AI in Praxis
text:
Q + A


“What’s one question or concern you still have about using AI in your writing?”




Encourage quick clarifying questions from the group.

Keep responses concise; flag deeper issues for follow-up resources.

Transition line: “We’ll finish by capturing the key take-aways and linking them back to the outcomes we began with.”

Segment timing: 40 min Learning Outcomes linked: Reinforces LO #7 (Ethics & Attribution) and transitions to LO #8 (Workflow Design). Format: Plenary shareback + facilitated discussion Deliverable: Shared SOP statements and verbal Q&A

title: AI in Praxis
text:
LO #8: Wrap-Up & Reflection

Goal ( – Workflow Design):
Sketch your own AI-augmented academic workflow for your next project (submit for next meeting).
Task (5 min):
Create a quick workflow table with these elements:

Phase: Which part will you augment first? (e.g. literature review)
Tool choice: Which AI tool(s) will you test? (e.g. Elicit + Scite)
Checkpoint: How will you verify outputs? (e.g. Verify DOIs weekly)
Timeline: When will you run this and who will review it? (e.g. 2 weeks)

Closing reflection:

“Name one change you’ll make in how you use AI for your research this month.”




Explain that this final activity ties all outcomes together through a personal action plan.

Encourage students to be realistic—choose one manageable workflow phase to pilot.

Remind them to include verification and disclosure steps in their plan.

Ask for one volunteer to share their workflow idea aloud.

End with: “AI is here to support disciplined creativity and efficient scholarship—use it well, use it ethically.”

Thank everyone for participating and share the post-session survey link.

Segment timing: 88 – 90 min Learning Outcome linked: LO #8 (Workflow Design) Format: Individual reflection → optional short share Deliverable: Personal workflow plan (mini table)

title: Spotted Ape
text:
The Differentiator

The Differentiator

The Spotted Ape Advantage
We don’t just “do AI.” We apply Behavioural Centred Design to every agent interaction.


Behavioural Science
COM-B and BCD frameworks govern how agents engage audiences — turning statistical patterns into culturally resonant behaviour change.

A senior strategic execution team on subscription. No retainer bloat, no agency overhead. Integrated from day one.
